title: The Weekly Bottom Line
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: Markets were mixed, with the S&P 500 reaching a new high despite rising oil prices. The Federal Reserve held rates steady, and the U.S. economy grew by 2% in Q1
excerpt: Markets were mixed, with the S&P 500 reaching a new high despite rising oil prices. The Federal Reserve held rates steady, and the U.S. economy grew by 2% in Q1. Inflation pressures increased to a two-year high.

content.introduction: Financial markets navigated a week of mixed signals, with the S&P 500 achieving a new milestone despite persistent inflationary pressures and rising energy costs. The Federal Reserve maintained its accommodative stance, while the U.S. economy demonstrated modest growth in the first quarter, setting the stage for a complex economic landscape in the coming months.
body:
## Market Performance Amidst Economic Crosscurrents

The S&P 500 reached an all-time high of 4,280, defying broader market volatility and rising oil prices that threatened to dampen investor sentiment. Technology stocks led the advance, with the Nasdaq Composite gaining 1.8% for the week, while energy sector performance lagged amid concerns over demand destruction. Market breadth showed signs of deterioration, with declining issues outpacing advancing issues by a 1.3-to-1 margin, suggesting underlying market fragility beneath the index's headline performance. The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) remained elevated at 18.2, indicating persistent investor anxiety despite the market's advance.

## Federal Reserve Policy Amidst Shifting Economic Data

The Federal Reserve maintained its benchmark interest rate range at 5.25-5.50%, as anticipated by market participants, with Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell emphasizing the need for 'more good data' before considering rate cuts. The Fed's statement acknowledged that 'inflation remains elevated' but noted progress toward the 2% target, though core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) inflation remained sticky at 2.8%. Market pricing now suggests a 65% probability of a September rate cut, down from 75% just a month ago, as inflation pressures have proven more persistent than expected. The Fed's dot plot projections showed median expectations for two rate cuts in 2024, unchanged from previous forecasts, creating a potential divergence between market pricing and central bank guidance.

## Economic Growth and Inflation Dynamics

The U.S. economy expanded at a 2.0% annualized rate in the first quarter, according to the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA), a modest deceleration from the 3.4% growth rate in Q4 2023. Consumer spending, which accounts for approximately 70% of economic activity, grew at a solid 2.5% pace, though business investment showed signs of weakness with equipment spending declining 0.7%. Inflation pressures intensified, with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rising 0.4% month-over-month in April, bringing the year-over-year increase to 3.4%, the highest level since September 2021. Core CPI, which excludes volatile food and energy components, rose 0.3% for the month and 3.6% year-over-year, exceeding economist expectations and reinforcing concerns about inflation's persistence. The Producer Price Index (PPI) accelerated to 2.2% year-over-year, suggesting continued pricing pressure at the wholesale level that could eventually filter through to consumer prices.

## Sector Analysis and Market Implications

Technology and communication services led sector performance, with the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) gaining 2.4% for the week, driven by strong earnings from several mega-cap tech companies. In contrast, energy sector underperformance continued, with the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) declining 1.1% despite rising oil prices, as investors weighed demand concerns against supply constraints. Defensive sectors like utilities and consumer staples outperformed, gaining 1.7% and 1.3% respectively, as investors rotated toward less economically sensitive names. Credit markets showed signs of tightening, with the ICE BofA US High Yield Index yield spread widening by 8 basis points to 4.15%, indicating increasing risk aversion among fixed income investors. The bond market's reaction to inflation data was muted, with the 10-year Treasury yield remaining range-bound between 4.5% and 4.6%, suggesting that investors have largely priced in the current inflationary environment.

content.outlook:
Looking ahead, ## U.S.

Most major U.S. stock indexes finished the week higher, with several hitting record highs, as some generally positive economic data, ongoing strength in artificial intelligence (AI)-linked stocks, and upbeat earnings results helped offset continued uncertainty surrounding the U.S.-Iran conf. Market participants will continue to monitor developments closely for signals about the direction of economic indicators trends and their broader implications for portfolio allocation and risk management strategies.
